mnemonics.title: 2PL Phases
mnemonics.content: GROW-SHRINK: Get locks in GROW phase, release in SHRINK phase - can't grow after shrinking begins

mnemonics.title: Wait-Die vs Wound-Wait
mnemonics.content: OLD waits for YOUNG in Wait-Die; OLD Wounds YOUNG in Wound-Wait - remember: wait=willing to wait, wound=attack

mnemonics.title: ACID Properties
mnemonics.content: Atomicity: All or Nothing, Consistency: Rules obeyed, Isolation: Concurrent seems serial, Durability: Written to disk stays

mnemonics.title: Strict vs Rigorous 2PL
mnemonics.content: STRICT: Shared locks released at end; RIGOROUS: ALL locks released at end - rigorous is stricter than strict

acronyms.usage: A concurrency control protocol with growing and shrinking phases for lock management
Validates transactions at commit time rather than using locks during execution
Timestamps used in timestamp ordering protocols to enforce serializability
Protocol for ensuring atomic commit across distributed sites
Concurrency control using transaction timestamps instead of locks

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: growing phase (locks acquired) → turning point → shrinking phase (locks released). Show that once shrinking starts, no new locks.
Nodes = transactions, Edges = waiting for lock. Cycle = deadlock. For distributed: draw separate site graphs, then merge into global graph.
Show timestamp line with transactions T1, T2, T3 with their timestamps. Show read/write operations with RTS/WTS values being compared.
Two columns: Older transactions vs Younger transactions. Arrows show action: Old waits for Young in Wait-Die; Old wounds Young in Wound-Wait.

analogies.analogy: Like a shopping cart: you add items (acquire locks) in the store, then checkout and return cart (release locks). Can't add items after starting checkout.
Two people at different offices each holding a document the other needs - classic deadlock but across locations.
Like taking a book from library shelf, reading it, then at checkout checking if anyone else borrowed it - if yes, you don't get it and must try again.
Like two people depositing money in the same account - whether they go sequentially or together, final balance should be the same as sequential execution.
Like timestamp on editing a shared Google doc - the system always applies edits in timestamp order to maintain consistency.
